title: Torad Stories — Story Building Agent
spec:
## Requirements

### R1: Story Premise Intake
User provides initial concept: title, logline, genre, tone. System validates and stores the premise.

### R2: Narrative Arc Decomposition
Decompose the premise into narrative arcs: setup, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution. Each arc has themes, conflicts, and resolution conditions.

### R3: Character Generation
Generate characters from arc requirements. Each character has: name, role (protagonist/antagonist/supporting), motivations, arc (how they change), relationships to other characters.

### R4: World-Building
Generate world elements: setting, rules, history, factions. Each element connects to arcs and characters. Consistency validation across all world elements.

### R5: Iterative Refinement
User can edit any element mid-process. Edits propagate: changing a character's motivation may require arc adjustments. The system tracks dependencies and flags conflicts.

### R6: Story Bible Output
Produce a structured story bible (JSON) that a generation agent consumes to produce the final narrative. Bible includes: premise, arcs, characters, world, relationships, timeline.

## Data Flow
StoryPremise → NarrativeArcs[] → Characters[] → WorldElements[]
All elements → ConsistencyCheck → StoryBible
UserEdit → DependencyGraph → ConflictDetection → PropagatedUpdates

## Build Sequence
1. R1 (Premise) — foundation
2. R2 (Arcs) — depends on R1
3. R3 (Characters) + R4 (World) — depend on R2, can parallelize
4. R5 (Refinement) — depends on R2, R3, R4
5. R6 (Bible output) — depends on all

## Architecture
PostgreSQL for all story data. Each story element is a row with JSONB content. Dependency graph stored as edges between elements.
rejection:
## Anti-Patterns

### Do NOT generate the entire story in one pass
The system builds structure, not prose. Generation happens downstream in a separate agent.

### Do NOT flatten the dependency graph
If arc A depends on character B, that relationship must be explicit and queryable. Flattening into a single document loses the ability to propagate edits.

### Do NOT auto-resolve conflicts
When an edit creates a conflict, surface it to the user. Do not silently fix it — the conflict may be intentional (dramatic tension).

## Boundaries
- Premise intake is a command (creates, validates)
- Arc decomposition is domain logic (pure, no I/O)
- Character and world generation use LLM — they are tools with Output.object
- Consistency checking is a validator
- Edit propagation is domain logic with a dependency graph query
- Bible output is a transformer (structured data → JSON format)

## Drift Risks
- Generation logic creeping into the structure-building phase
- Consistency checking becoming a blocker instead of an advisor
- World-building becoming disconnected from arcs (world must serve the story)
additionalConstraints: Must support iterative refinement (user edits mid-process)
Story bible output must be JSON-serializable
